title_en: Abu Talib al-Makki and the Nourishment of Hearts
title_id: Abu Talib al-Makki dan Makanan Hati
content_id:
Qut al-Qulub — Makanan Hati — adalah salah satu karya etika spiritual Islam yang paling awal dan komprehensif serta pendahulu langsung dari Ihya Ulum ad-Din al-Ghazali. Pengarangnya, Abu Talib Muhammad ibn Ali al-Makki (wafat 996 M), adalah seorang ulama Mekah dan guru spiritual yang menghabiskan hidupnya di kota-kota suci dan di Basra, di mana ia memiliki pengaruh yang signifikan sebelum pindah ke Baghdad. Ia termasuk dalam generasi ulama Sufi awal yang meletakkan fondasi bagi artikulasi sistematis ilmu-ilmu spiritual Islam.

Buku ini ditulis sebagai panduan komprehensif bagi kehidupan spiritual — mencakup amal-amal ibadah, dimensi-dimensi batinnya, keadaan-keadaan hati, dan jalan mukmin menuju kedekatan dengan Allah. Al-Makki mengambil dari tradisi hadis klasik dan kosakata para syaikh Sufi yang sedang berkembang untuk menyajikan akun terpadu tentang praktik dan spiritualitas Islam. Karyanya menonjol karena sintesisnya atas dimensi-dimensi legal dan spiritual Islam pada periode ketika keduanya terkadang diperlakukan sebagai domain yang terpisah.

Qut al-Qulub memiliki pengaruh mendalam pada al-Ghazali, yang mengakui utangnya kepadanya saat membangun Ihya. Banyak elemen struktural dari Ihya — organisasi empat bagiannya, pembahasan tentang penyakit-penyakit hati, pembahasan tentang kebajikan-kebajikan spiritual tertentu — menunjukkan jejak-jejak yang jelas dari karya al-Makki. Dalam pengertian ini, Qut al-Qulub menempati posisi penting dalam sejarah literatur spiritual Islam, berdiri di antara tulisan-tulisan Sufi paling awal dan sintesis komprehensif yang kemudian akan diproduksi al-Ghazali.

Karya ini lebih sulit dibaca daripada Ihya atau banyak teks spiritual berikutnya, sebagian karena ditulis pada periode ketika kosakata ilmu spiritual Islam masih berkembang dan distandardisasi, dan sebagian karena gaya al-Makki padat dan terkadang eliptis. Meskipun demikian, ia mengandung wawasan-wawasan yang mendalam dan merupakan sumber primer yang berharga untuk memahami bagaimana ilmu-ilmu spiritual terbentuk pada abad Islam keempat.
content_en:
Qut al-Qulub — The Nourishment of Hearts — is one of the earliest comprehensive works of Islamic spiritual ethics and a direct predecessor to al-Ghazali's Ihya Ulum ad-Din. Its author, Abu Talib Muhammad ibn Ali al-Makki (died 996 CE), was a Meccan scholar and spiritual teacher who spent his life in the holy cities and in Basra, where he had significant influence before moving to Baghdad. He belongs to the generation of early Sufi scholars who were laying the foundations for the systematic articulation of the Islamic spiritual sciences.

The book was written as a comprehensive guide to the spiritual life — covering acts of worship, their inner dimensions, the states of the heart, and the path of the believer toward nearness to Allah. Al-Makki drew on both the classical hadith tradition and the emerging vocabulary of the Sufi masters to present an integrated account of Islamic practice and spirituality. His work is notable for its synthesis of the legal and spiritual dimensions of Islam at a period when these were sometimes treated as separate domains.

Qut al-Qulub had a profound influence on al-Ghazali, who acknowledged his debt to it when constructing the Ihya. Many of the structural elements of the Ihya — its four-part organization, its treatment of the heart's diseases, its discussion of specific spiritual virtues — show clear traces of al-Makki's work. In this sense, Qut al-Qulub occupies a pivotal position in the history of Islamic spiritual literature, standing between the earliest Sufi writings and the comprehensive synthesis that al-Ghazali would later produce.

The work is more difficult to read than the Ihya or many subsequent spiritual texts, partly because it was written at a period when the vocabulary of Islamic spiritual science was still being developed and standardized, and partly because al-Makki's style is dense and sometimes elliptical. Nevertheless, it contains profound insights and is a valuable primary source for understanding how the spiritual sciences took shape in the fourth Islamic century.
